title: The Cross Explosions Foreshadowing
wikitext:
[]: The Cross Explosions = Foreshadowing?             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Fri Jan 15, 2010 3:15 am This topic got started ca. My Post Here where I replied saying that the cross explosions used throughout the series serve as foreshadowing for the events in EoE; Specifically in how crosses portend sacrifice, death, and rebirth and how we see all of this both on a literal/narrative and symbolic/metaphoric level in EoE. My argument (and counters to the counter-arguments against mine) follow: First, to define the term: "Foreshadowing is a literary technique used by many different authors to provide clues for the reader to be able to predict what might occur later on in the story. In other words, it is a literary device in which an author drops hints about the plot and what may come in the near future or, in other words, the plot developments to come later in the story."

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Fri Jan 15, 2010 6:35 am I think you've gotten it backward. The original cross-shaped explosions were a whole lot of PRS. The explosions/energy-crosses in EoE could simply have been chosen later to fit in with what had previously been established as a theme for a few of the Angels: cross-shaped explosions. They need not carry this connotation at all, and need not be a Chekov's gun. Besides, picking the later meaning of one form of a motif need not necessarily transpose its meaning onto an earlier instance, especially when the context in which both are used is significantly different.

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Fri Jan 15, 2010 12:34 pm Jimbo wrote: This obviously can't work in TV or anime.

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Fri Jan 15, 2010 1:26 pm Hideaki Anno wrote: I don't know what the result will be. That is because within me, the story is not yet finished.

[]:             Quote Post by JoeD80 » Fri Jan 15, 2010 2:52 pm I always thought Anno was referring to the characters and that he didn't know where he was taking them and how the events would shape them, not that he didn't know where the plot was supposed to go. A lot of the text in the proposal can be linked to what occurs in the series. Stuff like Rei's true nature is mentioned in other parts of the proposal, and Human Instrumentality and what that is is mentioned several times in the document. Details got shifted, but the show's plot was always going to revolve around Instrumentality, Artificial Evolution, and the Eva vs. Angel conflict. Details get refined as a show continues, e.g. changing the mental attack on Shinji to a mental attack on Asuka. Sometimes a writer gets a good idea far into production, but the main plot continues on as the details change around it.

[]:             Quote Post by Allemann » Fri Jan 15, 2010 4:06 pm Hideaki Anno wrote: I don't know what the result will be. That is because within me, the story is not yet finished.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri Jan 15, 2010 4:15 pm Allemann wrote: the episode where the name Lilith is first spoken.

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Fri Jan 15, 2010 4:46 pm Leaving aside the matter of episode #15, I'm not convinced that Lilith was entirely absent from the planned story in the beginning. Given the sheer amount of kabbalistic references and nomenclature in the show, it's clear that the creators were reasonably familiar with the material. A big point to note here is that the Angels start at three; Sachiel is the Third Angel. With Adam as the first, we're left with a glaring absence in the plot. With Eva already taken, it's not a stretch that Lilith was always meant to fill this hole. I'd extend this even further and state that the Rei==Lilith connection was probably present in the production script from day one. Does this mean that it was always meant to be Lilith hanging on the cross in Terminal Dogma, or that the ending was set in stone from the very beginning. Not in and of itself no. But I'm disinclined to think that the name Lilith was simply plucked from a kabbalistic tome for a last minute plot twist. Rather it was something which, like Kaworu, was always in the pipeline, and always intended to play a role in the plot.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Fri Jan 15, 2010 5:44 pm ObsessiveMathsFreak wrote: Leaving aside the matter of episode #15, I'm not convinced that Lilith was entirely absent from the planned story in the beginning.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Fri Jan 15, 2010 6:11 pm I'm not entirely clear on my nomenclature but hear me out on this... I remember clear as day my junior high literature class, in which we were reading and analyzing Romeo & Juliet. There is a point in Romeo & Juliet where it rains. The teacher told us that this rain was 'foreshadowing' the tragedy at the end of the story. She did not say 'motif', she definitely said 'foreshadowing'. By that same token, could not the fact that the crossplosions' shape reminds viewers of a grave marker be sufficient in itself to be able to call the crossplosions a foreshadowing device for the end of the world? We would be able to say this even if not a single cross appeared in EoE, the same way it wouldn't have to actually rain at the moment of Romeo & Juliet's death for the rain earlier in the story to constitute foreshadowing the tragedy to come. Rather, it is the mood the symbol evokes which is foreshadowing the future event by giving us an early taste of its mood.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Fri Jan 15, 2010 10:03 pm UrsusArctos wrote: (1) The original cross-shaped explosions were a whole lot of PRS. The explosions/energy-crosses in EoE could simply have been chosen later to fit in with what had previously been established as a theme for a few of the Angels: cross-shaped explosions. They need not carry this connotation at all, and need not be a Chekov's gun. (2) Besides, picking the later meaning of one form of a motif need not necessarily transpose its meaning onto an earlier instance, especially when the context in which both are used is significantly different.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sat Jan 16, 2010 1:30 am Building upon something created in frantic chaos results in a sense of foreshadowing to the viewer. It's always important not to question how a story element found it's way into the script. What's important is seeing what it does in that script. Otherwise, you get caught up in question how stories are made, rather than what stories do. And especially in a discussion that centers around what the story is doing , it's more important to look at that rather than how it got there in the first place. (Example: Nobody cares why George Lucas thought up of Jar Jar Binks. What matters is that he's in Phantom Menace and is ruining everything because of it!) Was foreshadowing the reason why the explosions were cross shaped? Probably not. Were the cross-shaped explosion later on used as a method of foreshadowing within in the story anyway? I believe so, yes.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sat Jan 16, 2010 1:54 am FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: Otherwise, you get caught up in question how stories are made, rather than what stories do.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sat Jan 16, 2010 2:15 am It obviously varies when you need to separate the story from the storyteller(s) involved. You're right about that quote. More time than not, I tend to interpret things better when I'm not constantly questioning whether or not the artist intended for the audience to think something specific. Because whether the artist meant for it or not, the audience may have thought something about the story anyway, and therefore it does effect the story, even if in a way the storyteller did not intend. SPOILER: Show

[]:             Quote Post by VoidEater » Wed Jan 20, 2010 6:34 pm I only see the crossplosions as communcating a non-mundane ("metabiological") dimension to the angels.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Thu Jan 21, 2010 6:10 am How has nobody brought up Tsurumaki's statement yet? Assistant Director Kazuya Tsurumaki wrote: There are a lot of giant robot shows in Japan, and we did want our story to have a religious theme to help distinguish us. Because Christianity is an uncommon religion in Japan we thought it would be mysterious. None of the staff who worked on Eva are Christians. There is no actual Christian meaning to the show, we just thought the visual symbols of Christianity look cool . If we had known the show would get distributed in the US and Europe we might have rethought that choice.

[]:             Quote Post by VoidEater » Thu Jan 21, 2010 1:45 pm ^...You're right, it's just a show about unresolved pubescent angst.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Thu Jan 21, 2010 2:06 pm NAveryW wrote: Anno is also commonly quoted as saying the use of religious symbols was "pedantry", but I don't remember where.
